What I bring to this role is seven years in railway signaling.
It was in San Francisco that I first ran a full commissioning cycle.
What sets me apart is that I read the standard and I run the site.
The reason I moved into signaling is that safety is where engineering becomes non-negotiable.
Thank you for making the time, João. Twenty minutes, and I would rather use them well. So, tell me about yourself.

Let me set out my background in three steps. What I do at Motiva is own the signaling design for metro line extensions, from the specification to commissioning. What I bring to this role is seven years of managing German, French and Chinese suppliers under contractual pressure.
Good. Everyone on this shortlist has seven years. What makes you the right person, and not the other four?

What sets me apart is that I read the standard and I run the site. Most engineers do one or the other. It was in San Francisco, in 2023, that I did both on the same project: two months of field commissioning, with the client watching every test.
Tell me about a conflict with an international supplier. Not the theory. One project, and what you decided.

One example that comes to mind is a supplier who missed three consecutive deadlines on a signaling package. What I did was put forward a recovery plan before anyone mentioned the penalty clause. What I learned from that experience was that a corrective action with a date is worth more than an apology. That is stakeholder management, and it is most of the job.

One more, and it is the uncomfortable one. You told us there was a period when you were not using English intensively. This role is in English, every day, with a regulator on the call. How do I know that will not be a problem?

I can account for it. For two years I was on site, in Portuguese, every day, and the language went quiet. What I learned from that was that fluency is a habit, not a memory. It was an interview, in March, that made me rebuild the habit deliberately: weekly training, technical reading, and this conversation. I did not throw in the towel. I would say my greatest strength in this area is exactly that: resilience.
Let me set out my background in three steps.
What I bring to this role is seven years of managing international suppliers.
One example that comes to mind is a supplier who missed three consecutive deadlines.
I can account for it, and what I learned was that fluency is a habit, not a memory.

João, this is Sofie Claes. I said I would call you after the panel, and I would rather you heard this from me than from a recruiter. First, the decision. We are taking you to the final round, and the two people who were most difficult to convince are the two who are now most in favor. Second, why. What convinced the panel was not the seven years, it was the way you set out the San Francisco commissioning. You told us what the problem was, what you decided, and what it cost. That is a track record, and very few candidates give us one. Third, the part you will want to hear and will not enjoy. Under pressure, you retreat into detail. When my technical director asked you about competing priorities, you gave him three minutes of relay logic. What he wanted was one sentence about how you decide what to drop. In the final round, the questions will be less technical and more uncomfortable, and the temptation will be to burn the midnight oil on the specification. Do not. Prepare the four sentences that make your value proposition, and prepare the one about the gap in your English, because I will ask you about it, and I will ask you in front of the board. You will not throw in the towel. You did not throw in the towel today.

Before your next interview in English, three things, because engineers get these wrong more often than anybody else. First. Nobody is testing your English. They are testing whether the engineering survives the English. A hiring manager can forgive a wrong preposition. What she cannot forgive is not knowing what you actually did on the project, and a candidate who buries the decision inside the description is a candidate who did not make the decision. Set out the problem, the choice, the outcome. Three sentences. Second. Cleft sentences are the cheapest confidence you can buy. Instead of saying I have experience with international suppliers, say what I bring to this role is seven years of managing international suppliers under contractual pressure. The information is identical. The weight is not. It buys you half a second to think, and it tells the panel where to look. Third, and this is the one that costs people the job. When you do not know, say so, and then say what you would do to find out. A subject matter expert is not somebody who knows everything. It is somebody the team trusts when the answer is not in the manual. And one last word. Every panel has one question designed to make you uncomfortable. It is not a trap. It is the whole interview. Prepare that answer, out loud, before you sit down.
